reasoning:
Both responses are strong and cover all required sections, but they optimize differently. Response A is more operationally minimal and internally simple; Response B is more cryptographically rigorous and threat-aware.

On encryption, B is stronger: it explicitly names and structures Signal-style X3DH + Double Ratchet with IK/SPK/OPK lifecycle and gives a fuller group-key story. A is solid but somewhat hand-wavy in handshake details (prekey use and authentication are less precisely specified).

On architecture fitness for a single VPS/non-specialist admin, A is better right-sized: Go monolith + Postgres + Caddy + local storage is exactly the target complexity. B is still viable but drifts toward optional extras (MinIO/Redis/Prometheus/Loki/Nginx+Certbot stack), which increases operational surface.

Invite system: B is stronger due to the hash-of-secret token design with URL fragment secrecy (server does not receive raw token before redemption), signed invite creation, and clear audit-chain persistence. A is good but more conventional server-generated token flow with weaker privacy nuance.

Security model: B is slightly stronger because it more explicitly discusses active server attacks (key substitution MITM) and mitigation (safety numbers), plus clearer limitations and availability risks. A is also good, but less explicit on active MITM defenses.

Self-hosting practicality: A wins for clarity and deployability by a competent non-specialist; fewer moving parts and a clean Docker Compose story. B has good ops detail but is more complex and has minor coherence issues around optional dependencies.

Design depth/precision: A is slightly more coherent end-to-end (schema, routing, deployment all align simply). B is very detailed but has a few inconsistencies/overcomplications relative to requirements.

Overall, B wins because the prompt’s hardest requirements are invite-chain security/privacy and server-blind E2EE correctness, where B is stronger, even though A is better operationally minimalist.
